vulnerability.title: Verify Permissions on cron.weekly
vulnerability.description: To properly set the permissions of /etc/cron.weekly, run the command: $ sudo chmod 0700 /etc/cron.weekly
vulnerability.recommendation: Remediation script: # Remediation is applicable only in certain platforms if rpm --quiet -q kernel-core; then find -H /etc/cron.weekly/ -maxdepth 0 -perm /u+s,g+xwrs,o+xwrt -type d -exec chmod u-s,g-xwrs,o-xwrt {} \; else >&2 echo 'Remediation is not applicable, nothing was done' fi

triage_decision.reason: Setting /etc/cron.weekly to 0700 is a standard file permission hardening that does not affect authentication, networking, or critical services.

previous_attempts.scan_output: Single-rule scan failed for file_permissions_cron_weekly (rule=xccdf_org.ssgproject.content_rule_file_permissions_cron_weekly). Could not verify.

previous_review_verdicts.feedback: The plan correctly addresses the vulnerability by enforcing 0700 permissions on /etc/cron.weekly. It is safe and straightforward, though it lacks some niceties such as pre‑check, audit, and logging.
concerns: Lacks a pre‑check for the directory’s existence and any existing special permissions. Not backing up current permissions. No verification of successful permission change.
suggested_improvements: Add a test to confirm /etc/cron.weekly exists before running chmod. Back up current permissions (e.g., chmod --reference or store the mode value). Verify the final permissions match 0700 and log the action. Consider handling symlinks or special setuid/setgid cases to avoid unintended exposure.
